summary: Joel Spolsky announces his retirement from traditional blogging on the 10-year anniversary of Joel on Software. He reflects on his experience writing for Inc. magazine, where editorial rewrites made pieces feel unlike his own voice. He declares the opinionated tech essay genre—Hacker News fodder—has become boring to him personally, though he acknowledges it remains a valid format for others. He calls for a shift from recycled conventional wisdom toward more objective, measurable knowledge that fills in the 'long tail' rather than repeating the same ideas. He also announces ending the StackOverflow podcast's current format, closing the Joel on Software discussion group, and sharply curtailing speaking engagements.
key_points: The opinionated tech essay format, pioneered by Greenspun and Winer, has been so widely adopted that it now feels exhausted and repetitive
The tech community keeps recycling the same ideas (fail fast, NDAs are worthless, execution over ideas) instead of producing genuinely new, objective knowledge
Professional editing at Inc. improved his writing technically but stripped away his authentic voice, making the columns feel like someone else's work
The next decade should focus on measurable truth rather than anecdotes, and on filling the long tail of knowledge rather than rehashing familiar arguments
Twitter is dismissed as a medium that reduces complex thought to context-free sentence fragments
money_quotes: After a decade of this, the whole genre of Hacker News fodder is just too boring to me personally.
We need something that is more objective (based on measurable truth and falseness rather than just lists of anecdotes about successful projects and failed projects).

We need to stop rewriting the same things again and again (fail fast! NDAs are worthless! Execution matters, not ideas! Use the right tools for the job!). Instead we should start filling in the long tail of knowledge.
It creates a mentally stunted world in which the most complicated thought you can think is one sentence long.

Logging on gives you a page full of little hand grenades: impossible-to-understand, context-free sentences that take five minutes of research to unravel and which then turn out to be stupid, irrelevant, or pertaining to the television series Battlestar Galactica.
It's as if somebody kidnapped me and replaced me with an indistinguishable imposter who went to Columbia Journalism School.

I would write an essay describing why Twitter gives me a headache and makes me fear for the future of humanity, but it doesn't deserve more than 140 characters of explanation, and I've already spent 820.
key_insight: The tech blogging world has saturated itself with recycled conventional wisdom dressed as insight, and what's needed now is objective, measurable knowledge that fills gaps rather than repeating familiar mantras.
